title: L03 · Greetings + ser / estar / tener / haber
objective: Introduce yourself and survive a Spanish conversation. Master the FOUR most important verbs — including ser-vs-estar, the single biggest grammar pain point of Spanish.
strategy: Spanish splits 'to be' into TWO verbs (ser, estar) and uses 'tener' (to have) where English/French would use 'to be' (age, hunger, fear). Section 3-4 contrasts ser/estar with crisp examples. Section 5 covers the 'tener' idioms that surprise everyone.

sections.title: Greetings & courtesy
sections.note: Spanish uses different greetings throughout the day. Memorise the formal vs informal split.

blocks.translation: Hi / Salut — universal, any time of day
Good morning / Bonjour — until ~midday
Good afternoon / Bon après-midi — from midday to evening
Good evening / good night / Bonsoir, bonne nuit
How are you? (informal) / Comment vas-tu?
blocks.hint: Estás = from 'estar' (a temporary state). Not 'eres' here.

blocks.translation: How are you? (formal) / Comment allez-vous?
Fine, thanks. And you? / Bien, merci. Et toi?
Goodbye / Au revoir
See you later / À plus tard
See you tomorrow / À demain
Please / S'il te plaît
Thanks / You're welcome — Merci / De rien
Sorry / Pardon, Je suis désolé
Nice to meet you / Enchanté

sections.title: ser — to be (essence, identity, origin)
sections.note: SER tells you WHAT or WHO something IS — permanent identity, origin, profession, characteristic. Memorise this table; it's irregular and you'll use it daily.

blocks.translation: to be (essence) / être
blocks.rule: Highly irregular. Use SER for: (1) identity — 'Soy Daniel'. (2) origin — 'Soy de España'. (3) profession — 'Es médico'. (4) inherent characteristic — 'Ella es alta'. (5) time/date — 'Son las tres'.

examples.translation: I am Ana. / Je suis Ana. — identity
I'm from Mexico. / Je suis du Mexique. — origin
She is a doctor. / Elle est médecin. — profession (NO 'una' before profession)
My brother is tall. / Mon frère est grand. — inherent trait
It's 3 o'clock. / Il est 3 heures. — time always uses SER

sections.title: estar — to be (state, location, temporary condition)
sections.note: ESTAR tells you HOW something IS RIGHT NOW or WHERE it is. State, mood, location, ongoing actions.

blocks.translation: to be (state, location) / être
blocks.rule: Use ESTAR for: (1) location — 'Estoy en casa'. (2) temporary state/mood — 'Estoy cansado'. (3) ongoing action with gerund — 'Estoy comiendo'. (4) result of a change — 'La puerta está cerrada'. Notice: 'estar' has a written accent on tú/él/ellos forms.

examples.translation: I'm at home. / Je suis à la maison. — location ALWAYS uses ESTAR
I'm tired. / Je suis fatigué. — temporary mood
I'm eating (right now). / Je suis en train de manger. — present continuous
The door is closed. / La porte est fermée. — result/state
Where is the bathroom? / Où sont les toilettes? — location

sections.title: ser vs estar — the contrast
sections.note: THIS is the famous one. Same English word 'to be', two Spanish verbs. The choice CHANGES meaning. Drill these contrast pairs.

blocks.text: ser bueno (a good person) vs estar bueno (tastes good / hot)
blocks.audioText: Él es bueno. La sopa está buena.
blocks.rule: 'ser + adjective' = inherent trait. 'estar + adjective' = current state. With 'bueno' the difference flips moral vs sensory.

examples.translation: He is a good person. / C'est quelqu'un de bon.
The soup tastes good. (right now) / La soupe est bonne (au goût).

blocks.audioText: El libro es aburrido. Estoy aburrido.
blocks.rule: Classic trap. SER aburrido = the thing IS boring (inherent). ESTAR aburrido = someone IS bored (state).

examples.translation: The book is boring. / Le livre est ennuyeux.
I'm bored. / Je m'ennuie.

blocks.rule: Same adjective, totally different meaning depending on the verb.

examples.translation: She is very clever. / Elle est très intelligente.
I'm ready to leave. / Je suis prêt à partir.

blocks.text: LOCATION = always ESTAR. EVENT location = SER.
blocks.audioText: Madrid está en España. La fiesta es en mi casa.
blocks.rule: A physical thing's location → ESTAR. An EVENT's location (where it takes place) → SER. Counterintuitive but consistent.

examples.translation: Madrid is in Spain. (location) / Madrid est en Espagne.

examples.text: La fiesta es en mi casa.
examples.audioText: La fiesta es en mi casa.
examples.translation: The party is at my house. (event) / La fête est chez moi.

examples.text: El concierto es a las ocho.
examples.audioText: El concierto es a las ocho.
examples.translation: The concert is at 8 (= takes place). / Le concert est à 8 heures.

blocks.audioText: ser: Description, Occupation, Characteristic, Time, Origin, Relation. estar: Position, Location, Action, Condition, Emotion.
blocks.rule: SER → DOCTOR: Description (tall, pretty), Occupation, Characteristic (kind, smart), Time/Date, Origin, Relation. ESTAR → PLACE: Position, Location, Action (-ing), Condition (sick, tired), Emotion (happy, sad).

examples.text: Soy alto y soy de Madrid.
examples.audioText: Soy alto y soy de Madrid.
examples.translation: I'm tall and I'm from Madrid. — both DOCTOR (description, origin)

examples.text: Estoy en Madrid y estoy feliz.
examples.audioText: Estoy en Madrid y estoy feliz.
examples.translation: I'm in Madrid and I'm happy. — both PLACE (location, emotion)

sections.title: tener — to have (and many surprising idioms)
sections.note: Stem-changing verb (e → ie). The shocker: Spanish uses TENER (to have) where English uses 'to be' for age, hunger, fear, heat, cold, sleep, thirst.

blocks.translation: to have / avoir
blocks.rule: Stem changes e→ie in singular and 3rd plural. 'yo' is irregular (tengo).

examples.translation: I have a dog. / J'ai un chien.
Do you have siblings? / As-tu des frères et sœurs?

blocks.text: TENER + noun = state expressed with 'to be' in English/French
blocks.audioText: Tengo 25 años. Tengo hambre. Tengo frío. Tengo miedo.
blocks.rule: Spanish HAS hunger/fear/cold instead of BEING hungry/afraid/cold. French speakers: identical to French 'avoir faim, avoir peur, avoir froid, avoir x ans'. English speakers: this is the structure you need to UNLEARN.

examples.translation: I am 25 years old. / J'ai 25 ans. — French speakers: same as French!
I'm hungry. (lit. I have hunger.) / J'ai faim.
I'm thirsty. / J'ai soif.
I'm cold. / J'ai froid.
I'm hot. / J'ai chaud.
I'm sleepy. / J'ai sommeil.
I'm afraid. / J'ai peur.
I'm in a hurry. / Je suis pressé.
I'm right. / J'ai raison.

sections.title: haber — auxiliary 'to have' + the magic word 'hay'
sections.note: HABER is mostly an auxiliary verb (used to form perfect tenses later). For now you only need ONE form: HAY = there is / there are.

blocks.text: hay = there is / there are (INVARIABLE — same for sing & plur)
blocks.audioText: Hay un libro. Hay tres libros. ¿Hay un baño aquí?
blocks.rule: 'hay' (from haber) is invariable: 'hay un libro' (there is a book), 'hay tres libros' (there are three books). Used for EXISTENCE. Unlike 'estar' (location of a SPECIFIC thing), 'hay' introduces something new or asks if something exists.

examples.text: Hay un libro en la mesa.
examples.audioText: Hay un libro en la mesa.
examples.translation: There is a book on the table. / Il y a un livre sur la table.
There are three books here. / Il y a trois livres ici.
Is there a bathroom nearby? / Y a-t-il des toilettes près d'ici?

examples.text: En España hay 47 millones de personas.
examples.audioText: En España hay cuarenta y siete millones de personas.
examples.translation: There are 47 million people in Spain. / Il y a 47 millions de personnes en Espagne.

blocks.text: hay vs está/están — existence vs specific location
blocks.audioText: Hay un libro en la mesa. El libro está en la mesa.
blocks.rule: 'hay' introduces a NEW or UNSPECIFIED thing ('there's a book somewhere'). 'está/están' locates a SPECIFIC, known thing ('the book is on...'). Trigger: 'un/una/dos/tres + noun' = hay. 'el/la/mi + noun' = está.

examples.text: Hay un perro en la calle.
examples.audioText: Hay un perro en la calle.
examples.translation: There is a (some) dog in the street. (new) / Il y a un chien dans la rue.

examples.text: El perro está en la calle.
examples.audioText: El perro está en la calle.
examples.translation: The dog is in the street. (specific, known) / Le chien est dans la rue.

sections.title: A real introduction
sections.note: Listen to the whole dialogue using 'Play section', then work through it line by line.

blocks.text: Hola, me llamo Marta. ¿Cómo te llamas?
blocks.audioText: Hola, me llamo Marta. Cómo te llamas?
blocks.translation: Hi, my name is Marta. What's your name? / Salut, je m'appelle Marta. Comment t'appelles-tu?
I'm Daniel. Nice to meet you. / Je m'appelle Daniel. Enchanté.
Where are you from? / D'où viens-tu? — note: SER for origin

blocks.text: Soy de Francia, pero vivo en Madrid.
blocks.audioText: Soy de Francia, pero vivo en Madrid.
blocks.translation: I'm from France, but I live in Madrid. / Je suis de France, mais je vis à Madrid.
How old are you? / Quel âge as-tu? — TENER for age
I'm 28. And you? / J'ai 28 ans. Et toi?
